# Semantic catalog

<!-- This file is generated by docs/generate-semantic-catalog.js. Edit the generator and semantics/*.json, then rerun npm run docs:semantic-catalog. -->

This document explains every built-in canonical semantic recognized by the plugin.
It complements the usage guide in the README.

Japanese version: [semantic-catalog_ja.md](semantic-catalog_ja.md).

## Policy

- The source of truth for names, aliases, tags, and default attributes is `semantics/en.json` plus locale aliases such as `semantics/ja.json`.
- English canonical names are always recognized. Japanese aliases are loaded by default through the `languages` option.
- A canonical semantic is the stable output concept and API name. Labels and aliases are input headings that select that canonical output contract.
- Built-in aliases may use regular-expression patterns. Runtime `semanticContainerSc` aliases are literal strings.
- The package emits stable `sc-*` classes for all semantics.
- This plugin handles semantics that wrap content as `section`, `aside`, `div`, or `nav`.
- Built-in titlepage inference converts conservative numbered, lettered, and Roman-numeral hr-sandwiched `h1` headings into `chapter-titlepage`, `appendix-titlepage`, and `part-titlepage`. Parsed frontmatter can also set `sc.titlepage: true` or nested `sc: { titlepage: true }` to wrap from the first content `h1` without an opening body `hr`; no plugin option is required for that control.
- Explicit titlepage labels are supported as direct semantic labels, but they are marker-like compared with natural document labels. Prefer `h1` inference or `sc.titlepage: true` for ebook title pages; explicit labels remain visible unless hidden with `labelControl` or `semanticContainerSc`, and hidden labels on roleless `div` titlepages do not add an `aria-label` fallback. If a workflow needs accessible named titlepage groups, add an explicit role-bearing wrapper such as `role="group"` in that workflow.
- `Prologue`, `Epilogue`, `Introduction`, `Conclusion`, `序章`, `終章`, `プロローグ`, and `エピローグ` are not inferred as h1 titlepages by default. Use explicit semantic labels for those DPUB section semantics, or handle whole-document wrapping in EPUB-level tooling.
- Figure-like examples are intentionally delegated to figure/caption plugins such as `p7d-markdown-it-figure-with-p-caption`.
- `role="doc-*"` is emitted only for exact or close DPUB-ARIA matches. EPUB structural terms and DPUB-ARIA roles are separate layers: matching an EPUB term does not by itself justify a role.
- Alias recognition is semantic classification, not cosmetic spelling normalization. Every built-in or runtime alias inherits its canonical semantic's tag, classes, and default attributes, including `role`. There are no alias-level role overrides; leave an ambiguous label unregistered or map it to an appropriate roleless semantic instead of attaching it to a role-bearing canonical.
- `doc-notice` is a structural document role whose superclass is `note`, not the live-region `role="alert"`; it does not by itself create a pop-up, move focus, or request immediate announcement.
- The plugin does not synthesize IDs or `aria-labelledby` relationships for visible labels. When a document contains multiple instances of the same DPUB landmark role, downstream authoring or EPUB tooling must give them unique accessible names. Structure-specific requirements also remain outside this recognizer: footnote/endnote tooling should own the lists and references required by `doc-endnotes`, while downstream tooling must provide descendant entry lists for `doc-bibliography` and real descendant links for `doc-index` and `doc-toc`.
- `epub:type` is not emitted by default.
- Canonical grammatical number follows an authoritative external vocabulary when the semantic intentionally corresponds to one. Otherwise, use a singular conceptual name by default and keep natural alternate-number headings as aliases. Retain a plural canonical only for a lexicalized or deliberately aggregate concept such as `acknowledgments`, `endnotes`, `keywords`, `requirements`, or `resources`; do not rename an established canonical merely for visual consistency.
- A multiword alias that contains another canonical name does not automatically belong to its grammatical head noun. Choose the canonical whose tag, role, and class best match the whole heading. If two output contracts are equally plausible, leave the phrase unregistered. Ambiguous registered choices are explained in the entry notes.
- Register an alias only when it is plausible as a standalone heading in real target documents, has a stable boundary from neighboring semantics, and leads to an appropriate output contract. Dictionary correspondence alone is insufficient; a rare but precise established heading can be safer than a common ambiguous word.

## Common distinctions

This section explains boundaries between easily confused semantics and shows which label to choose when several terms look similar.

- `answer` vs `solution`: `answer` is a direct response. `solution` can include a worked method or explanation.
- `question` vs `problem`: `problem`/`exercise` is a unit to solve and can contain its prompt directly; it does not require a nested `question`. `question` marks an independent question, subquestion, or teaching prompt. Matching numbers do not create automatic links to `answer` or `explanation` blocks.
- `interview` vs `qna`: `interview` identifies the editorial content type and stays roleless because its structure may mix narrative or conversation. Use `qna` when the section is explicitly a series of questions and answers and should emit `doc-qna`.
- `notice` vs `information`: current EPUB/DPUB `notice` notifies users of consequences that might arise from an action or event and emits `doc-notice`; Japanese `通知` / `通告` map here as direct author-selected headings. `information` presents general, background, or supplemental information without asserting that role; generic announcement headings `お知らせ` / `告知` therefore map to roleless `information`. Japanese `注意書` / `注意書き` map to `caution`; bare `掲示` remains unregistered.
- `information` vs `reference` vs `resources`: `information` presents useful facts or guidance in the block (`参考情報`). `reference` identifies what or where to consult (`参照`, `参照先`, `参照情報`). `resources` supplies supporting documents or assets (`参考資料`). Use `bibliography` for a formal references list and `related-link` for a see-also/further-reading link list.
- `book` vs `related-book` vs `colophon`: `book` is the canonical for an information block about a book, magazine, or publication and emits `sc-book`; `publication` is an input alias. `related-book` suggests related books, magazines, or publications and emits `sc-related-book`, with `related-publication` as an input alias. `colophon` is the current work's formal imprint.
- `preamble` vs `preface`/`foreword`/`introduction`/`prologue`: `preamble` is a formal opening statement. `preface` is authorial front matter, `foreword` is introductory front matter often by another contributor, `introduction` orients the main text, and `prologue` is an opening narrative or section.
- `afterword` vs `conclusion`: `afterword` is closing commentary from the author or another important contributor about the work's creation, significance, or later events. `conclusion` closes the subject, argument, or narrative itself. Japanese `あとがき`/`後書き` map to `afterword`; `おわりに`/`終わりに` and `結論` map to `conclusion`.
- `outline` vs `overview` vs `abstract` vs `summary`: `outline` exposes the main points or planned structure, often as a skeleton. `overview` gives broad orientation without detail. `abstract` is a concise representation of a larger work and emits `doc-abstract`. `summary` is a general condensation or recap and stays roleless because its position and function are not known from the heading alone. Japanese `要旨`/`抄録` are the role-bearing abstract labels; broader `概要`, `概略`, `要約`, and `まとめ` remain roleless.
- `chapter-titlepage`/`appendix-titlepage`/`part-titlepage` vs `chapter-toc`/`toc`: titlepage semantics wrap chapter/appendix/part opening design material. TOC semantics wrap navigational lists.
- `note` vs `notice`/`alert`/`important`: `note` is neutral and emits no default `role`. `notice` and consequence-oriented `alert` blocks emit structural `doc-notice`; this is not the live-region `role="alert"`. `important` marks emphasis without necessarily implying consequences and therefore emits no default role.
- `point` vs `hint`/`tip`: `point` states a central idea or takeaway and remains a roleless main-flow section. `hint` and `tip` provide helpful advice and emit `doc-tip` on an `aside`.
- Compound label ownership: `important notice` and `important information` map to roleless `important` because priority is the callout intent and the phrase alone does not guarantee the consequence-oriented `doc-notice` contract. `related information` and `related resources` map to `related` because the relationship makes the block tangential; unmodified `information` and `resources` remain main-flow sections. These are whole-heading output-contract decisions, not a general rule that modifiers always win.
- `caution` vs `warning` vs `danger`: `caution` is for care-needed points or preventable trouble. `warning` is stronger. `danger` is reserved for the strongest hazard level.
- `alert` vs `caution`: `alert` is a severity-neutral call for attention to a risk or problem; `caution` is a concrete care-needed point or instruction. Japanese `注意喚起` maps to `alert`, while `注意`, `注意事項`, `注意書`, and `注意書き` map to `caution`. Both are close mappings to the umbrella structural role `doc-notice`.
- `requirements` vs `prerequisites` vs `procedure` vs `limitations`: `requirements` are conditions to satisfy. `prerequisites` are preconditions before starting. `procedure` is how to do something. `limitations` are restrictions or constraints.
- `agenda` vs `issue`: `agenda` is the list of meeting or class topics. `issue` is a point to discuss, dispute, or resolve.
- `postscript` vs `errata` vs `updates`: `postscript` is an added after-note. `errata` is corrections after publication and emits `doc-errata`. `updates` is general update notes or revision history and emits no default role.
- `minutes` vs `agenda` vs `decision`: `agenda` is the plan for a meeting. `minutes` is the record of a meeting. `decision` is a decision item.
- `planning` vs `proposal` vs `suggestion` vs `recommendation`: `planning` describes plans or schedules. `proposal` is a formal plan or document submitted for approval. `suggestion` is a lighter idea. `recommendation` advises a choice or action.
- `learning-objective` vs `rubric` vs `assessment` vs `evaluation` vs `feedback`: `learning-objective` states learning goals. `rubric` states criteria. `assessment` measures or tests. `evaluation` judges quality, value, results, or performance. `feedback` comments on results. Japanese `評価`, `評価結果`, and `総合評価` map to the roleless `evaluation`; assessment-like forms, grading-like forms, and criteria labels remain separate or unregistered.
- `appendix` vs `supplement` vs `postscript`: `appendix` is a formal appended structure and emits `doc-appendix`. `supplement` clarifies or supplements the work and emits no default role. `postscript` is a later after-note. Japanese `補遺` maps to `supplement`, `追記` maps to `postscript`, and addendum-like `追補` remains unregistered.

## Entries

### Publishing structure and front/back matter

- `abstract`: Use for an abstract or short formal summary of a larger work.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-abstract" role="doc-abstract">`
  - English labels: `abstract`
  - Japanese labels: `要旨`, `抄録`
- `acknowledgments`: Use for acknowledgments or thanks sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-acknowledgments" role="doc-acknowledgments">`
  - English labels: `acknowledgments`, `acknowledgement`, `acknowledgment`, `acknowledgements`
  - Japanese labels: `謝辞`
- `afterword`: Use for an afterword placed after the main content.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-afterword" role="doc-afterword">`
  - English labels: `afterword`
  - Japanese labels: `後書き`, `あとがき`, `跋文`
- `appendix`: Use for appendices and attached supporting sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-appendix" role="doc-appendix">`
  - English labels: `appendix`, `appendices`
  - Japanese labels: `付録`, `付属書`, `附属書`, `付属資料`, `附属資料`, `(付録|付属|附属)[0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚａ-ｚ一二三四五六七八九十百千]{1,8}`
- `appendix-titlepage`: Use for ebook appendix-opening title pages, usually detected from an `h1` appendix heading.
  - Output: `<div class="sc-appendix-titlepage">`
  - English labels: `appendix-titlepage`, `appendix titlepage`, `appendix title page`
  - Japanese labels: `付録扉`, `付録タイトルページ`, `付属扉`, `付属タイトルページ`, `附属扉`, `附属タイトルページ`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted. For ebook title pages, prefer hr-sandwiched `h1` inference such as `Appendix A. Reference Data`, or parsed frontmatter `sc.titlepage: true` / `sc: { titlepage: true }`. The Policy section explains explicit-label visibility, hidden-label behavior, and accessible named groups shared by all titlepage semantics.
- `author`: Use for author information.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-author">`
  - English labels: `author`
  - Japanese labels: `著者`
- `bibliography`: Use for bibliographies, references lists, or works-cited sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-bibliography" role="doc-bibliography">`
  - English labels: `bibliography`, `references`, `reference list`, `works cited`
  - Japanese labels: `(参考)?文献(一覧)?`, `文献表`
  - Notes: Plural `References` maps here; singular `Reference` remains `reference`. `doc-bibliography` requires at least one descendant list of entries. The plugin recognizes the author-supplied section label but does not invent or validate that list structure.
- `book`: Use for book, magazine, or publication information blocks.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-book">`
  - English labels: `book`, `book information`, `book info`, `magazine information`, `magazine info`, `magazine`, `publication`, `publication information`, `publication info`
  - Japanese labels: `(書籍|雑誌)(情報|案内)?`, `書誌(情報)?`, `(出版物|刊行物)(情報|案内)`
  - Notes: This is the canonical semantic for book, magazine, and publication information and emits `sc-book`. `publication`, `magazine`, and their information forms are input labels. Use `related-book` for related or suggested publications and `colophon` for the current work's formal imprint. Bare plural `publications` is intentionally not recognized because it often means an author's works or a publication list.
- `chapter-toc`: Use for a table of contents scoped to a chapter or section.
  - Output: `<nav class="sc-chapter-toc" role="doc-toc">`
  - English labels: `chapter-toc`, `chapter toc`
  - Japanese labels: `章目次`
  - Notes: Emits `doc-toc` on `nav`. Use only when the wrapped content is actually a navigational list of links scoped to a chapter or smaller section; the plugin does not manufacture or validate link structure.
- `chapter-titlepage`: Use for ebook chapter-opening title pages, usually detected from an `h1` chapter heading.
  - Output: `<div class="sc-chapter-titlepage">`
  - English labels: `chapter-titlepage`, `chapter titlepage`, `chapter title page`
  - Japanese labels: `章扉`, `章タイトルページ`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted. For ebook title pages, prefer hr-sandwiched `h1` inference such as `Chapter 1. Title`, or parsed frontmatter `sc.titlepage: true` / `sc: { titlepage: true }`. The Policy section explains explicit-label visibility, hidden-label behavior, and accessible named groups shared by all titlepage semantics.
- `colophon`: Use for colophon/publication-information sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-colophon" role="doc-colophon">`
  - English labels: `colophon`
  - Japanese labels: `奥付`
- `conclusion`: Use for conclusions that close the subject, argument, or narrative.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-conclusion" role="doc-conclusion">`
  - English labels: `conclusion`
  - Japanese labels: `終わりに`, `おわりに`, `結び`, `結論`
- `credits`: Use for credits sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-credits" role="doc-credits">`
  - English labels: `credits`
  - Japanese labels: `クレジット`
- `dedication`: Use for dedication sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-dedication" role="doc-dedication">`
  - English labels: `dedication`
  - Japanese labels: `献呈`
- `editor-note`: Use for editorial notes, not general reader notes.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-editor-note">`
  - English labels: `editor-note`, `editor note`, `editor's note`, `editors' note`, `editorial note`
  - Japanese labels: `編注`, `編集注`, `編集者注`
  - Notes: Natural spaced forms are recognized. Unspaced `editornote` and apostrophe-less `editors note` are intentionally not treated as English headings.
- `endnotes`: Use for endnotes or collected notes at the end of a chapter or book.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-endnotes" role="doc-endnotes">`
  - English labels: `endnotes`
  - Japanese labels: `後注`, `章末注`, `巻末注`
  - Notes: `doc-endnotes` requires at least one descendant list of notes and must not be applied directly to that list. This plugin only recognizes an explicitly authored section label; note collection, backlinks, list generation, and structural validation belong to a footnote/endnote plugin or downstream EPUB tooling.
- `epigraph`: Use for epigraphs or opening quotations.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-epigraph" role="doc-epigraph">`
  - English labels: `epigraph`
  - Japanese labels: `題辞`, `題句`, `題言`
- `epilogue`: Use for epilogues.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-epilogue" role="doc-epilogue">`
  - English labels: `epilogue`
  - Japanese labels: `エピローグ`, `終幕`, `終章`
- `errata`: Use for errata or correction lists.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-errata" role="doc-errata">`
  - English labels: `errata`, `correction`, `corrections`, `corrigenda`
  - Japanese labels: `正誤表`, `(([0-9]+年)?[0-9]+月[0-9]+日)?訂正`
  - Notes: Has a close DPUB-ARIA match and emits `doc-errata`. Use for corrections after publication; use `updates` for general update or revision-history notes.
- `first-published`: Use for first-publication information.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-first-published">`
  - English labels: `first-published`, `first (published|publication)`
  - Japanese labels: `初出`
- `foreword`: Use for forewords.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-foreword" role="doc-foreword">`
  - English labels: `foreword`
  - Japanese labels: `((本書|日本語版)の)?(刊行|発行|発刊)に寄せて`
  - Notes: Japanese labels accept `刊行に寄せて` and forms scoped by `本書の` or `日本語版の`; malformed bare `の刊行に寄せて` is intentionally not recognized.
- `glossary`: Use for glossaries or term lists.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-glossary" role="doc-glossary">`
  - English labels: `glossary`, `glossary of terms`
  - Japanese labels: `用語集`, `用語一覧`
  - Notes: Has a close DPUB-ARIA match and emits `doc-glossary`.
- `index`: Use for index sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-index" role="doc-index">`
  - English labels: `index`
  - Japanese labels: `索引`
  - Notes: `doc-index` represents a navigational aid containing links to indexed subjects. The plugin recognizes the section label but does not manufacture or validate index entries or links.
- `introduction`: Use for introductions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-introduction" role="doc-introduction">`
  - English labels: `introduction`
  - Japanese labels: `序論`, `序説`, `はじめに`, `始めに`
- `keywords`: Use for keyword sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-keywords">`
  - English labels: `keywords`
  - Japanese labels: `キーワード`, `重要語`, `手がかり語`
- `lead`: Use for an opening lead/lede prose block.
  - Output: `<div class="sc-lead">`
  - English labels: `lead`, `lede`
  - Japanese labels: `リード(文)?`, `導入文`
  - Notes: This is the only built-in semantic with `hideLabel: true`. It uses a roleless `div`: a lead/lede is normally opening prose rather than a separately navigable document section, and `lead` is not an EPUB Structural Semantics Vocabulary term or DPUB-ARIA role. The hidden control marker therefore does not create a named `region` landmark. The marker is hidden by default in standard, bracket, and GitHub alert input. A non-empty inline label control overrides the default and shows a replacement label. An exact jointless heading such as `## Lead` is intentionally not matched, because hiding its only text would leave an empty heading. This follows the general invariant for any semantic whose catalog label is hidden by default; it is not a hard-coded `lead` matcher exception.
- `part-titlepage`: Use for ebook part-opening title pages, usually detected from an `h1` part heading.
  - Output: `<div class="sc-part-titlepage">`
  - English labels: `part-titlepage`, `part titlepage`, `part title page`
  - Japanese labels: `部扉`, `部タイトルページ`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted. For ebook title pages, prefer hr-sandwiched `h1` inference such as `Part 1. Title`, or parsed frontmatter `sc.titlepage: true` / `sc: { titlepage: true }`. The Policy section explains explicit-label visibility, hidden-label behavior, and accessible named groups shared by all titlepage semantics.
- `postscript`: Use for postscripts or dated additions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-postscript">`
  - English labels: `postscript`
  - Japanese labels: `(([0-9]+年)?[0-9]+月[0-9]+日)?追記`
- `preamble`: Use for a formal preamble, such as the opening statement of a policy, declaration, or specification.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-preamble">`
  - English labels: `preamble`
  - Japanese labels: `前文`
  - Notes: Use for a formal opening statement. Keep separate from `preface` (authorial front matter), `foreword` (introductory words often by another contributor), `introduction` (main-text orientation), and `prologue` (opening narrative or document section).
- `preface`: Use for prefaces.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-preface" role="doc-preface">`
  - English labels: `preface`
  - Japanese labels: `前書き`, `まえがき`
- `prologue`: Use for prologues.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-prologue" role="doc-prologue">`
  - English labels: `prologue`
  - Japanese labels: `プロローグ`, `序幕`, `序章`
- `pullquote`: Use for distinctively placed or highlighted quotations from the current content.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-pullquote" role="doc-pullquote">`
  - English labels: `pullquote`, `pull quote`, `pull-quote`
  - Japanese labels: `プル(・)?ク[オォ]ート`
  - Notes: The canonical spelling follows EPUB `pullquote` and DPUB-ARIA `doc-pullquote`; `pull quote` and `pull-quote` are input aliases. Output and canonical-name configuration use `sc-pullquote` / `pullquote`. DPUB-ARIA requires a duplicated presentational pullquote to be hidden from assistive technologies, but the plugin cannot determine whether marked text is the source occurrence or a duplicate, so it does not add `aria-hidden` automatically. Add it in a downstream transform when the rendered pullquote duplicates text elsewhere.
- `toc`: Use for a document-level table of contents.
  - Output: `<nav class="sc-toc" role="doc-toc">`
  - English labels: `toc`, `table of contents`, `contents`
  - Japanese labels: `目次`, `もくじ`
  - Notes: Emits `doc-toc` on `nav`. Use only for a real table-of-contents navigation aid; the plugin does not manufacture or validate descendant links.

### Notices, key points, tips, and sidebars

- `alert`: Use for broad alerts or calls for attention whose severity is not fixed.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-alert" role="doc-notice">`
  - English labels: `alert`, `alerts`
  - Japanese labels: `アラート`, `注意喚起`
  - Notes: Keep this for broad alert/attention-call sections; use `warning`, `caution`, or `danger` when the severity is clearer. Japanese `注意喚起` describes the act or category of calling attention rather than one specific caution level, so it remains here. `警報` is intentionally not recognized because it implies a stronger alarm level or an alarm-system topic.
- `annotation`: Use for annotations or marginal/editorial comments that accompany the main content.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-annotation">`
  - English labels: `annotation`
  - Japanese labels: `注釈`
- `caution`: Use for caution notices where readers should avoid mistakes, unsafe actions, or avoidable trouble.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-caution" role="doc-notice">`
  - English labels: `caution`, `cautions`
  - Japanese labels: `注意`, `注意事項`, `ご注意`, `注意点`, `注意書`, `注意書き`, `使用上の注意`, `留意事項`
  - Notes: Use for preventable mistakes or care-needed points. Use `warning` or `danger` when the risk is stronger.
- `column`: Use for columns or sidebar articles.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-column">`
  - English labels: `column`
  - Japanese labels: `コラム`
- `danger`: Use for danger notices with the strongest hazard level.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-danger" role="doc-notice">`
  - English labels: `danger`, `dangers`
  - Japanese labels: `危険`, `危険事項`
  - Notes: Use sparingly for the strongest hazard level.
- `hint`: Use for hints that help readers solve or understand something.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-hint" role="doc-tip">`
  - English labels: `hint`
  - Japanese labels: `ヒント`
- `important`: Use for content whose priority or importance needs emphasis.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-important">`
  - English labels: `important`, `important notice`, `important information`
  - Japanese labels: `重要(なこと|な事柄|な情報|な事項|情報|事項)?`
  - Notes: Use this for importance rather than hazard severity. Noun phrases such as `important notice` and `important information` are recognized; abstract `importance` is intentionally not recognized. No default role is emitted because importance alone does not imply the consequences described by `doc-notice`.
- `information`: Use for general, background, or supplemental information presented in the block.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-information">`
  - English labels: `information`, `info`
  - Japanese labels: `お知らせ`, `告知`, `(参考)?情報`, `インフォメーション`
  - Notes: Use for general, background, or supplemental information presented in the block. Japanese `お知らせ` and `告知` are pragmatic roleless matches for generic announcements that do not establish the consequence-oriented `doc-notice` contract. `参考情報` maps here because it normally supplies material for understanding or judgment; `参照情報` maps to `reference` when the block identifies what or where to consult. Bare `案内` and `ご案内` remain unregistered because they can identify information, procedures, events, products, facilities, or navigation; use a more specific built-in label or a project-local `semanticContainerSc` alias when the intended function is stable. Bare `掲示` also remains unregistered.
- `memo`: Use for memo sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-memo">`
  - English labels: `memo`
  - Japanese labels: `メモ`
- `note`: Use for neutral notes, annotations, or remarks.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-note">`
  - English labels: `note`
  - Japanese labels: `ノート`, `注`, `註`, `注記`, `備考`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted. Kept as a section-level document block because many notes are part of the main flow, not tangential asides. Use `notice`, `warning`, `caution`, `important`, or `danger` for notice-like alerts.
- `notice`: Use for consequence-oriented notices, such as warnings, cautions, and dangers.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-notice" role="doc-notice">`
  - English labels: `notice`
  - Japanese labels: `通知`, `通告`
  - Notes: Emits the structural DPUB-ARIA `doc-notice` role, whose current definition is to notify users of consequences that might arise from an action or event; examples include warnings, cautions, and dangers. Its superclass is `note`, not `alert`: the role does not by itself create a pop-up, move focus, or request immediate announcement. Japanese `通知` and the more formal `通告` are built in as direct, natural headings for this author-selected semantic, just as English `Notice` is. The content still needs to fit the consequence-oriented role. Broader announcement headings `お知らせ` and `告知` map instead to roleless `information`; `掲示` remains unregistered. Japanese `注意書` and `注意書き` map to `caution` rather than serving as translations of `notice`.
- `point`: Use for a central point, key point, main point, or important takeaway.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-point">`
  - English labels: `point`, `key point`, `key points`, `main point`, `main points`
  - Japanese labels: `ポイント`, `要点`
  - Notes: English `point` can mean a central idea or the main substance of an argument, so Japanese `要点` remains here. It is a roleless main-flow `section`, not a `doc-tip` aside; use `hint` or `tip` for helpful advice.
- `tip`: Use for tips or practical advice.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-tip" role="doc-tip">`
  - English labels: `tip`, `tips`
  - Japanese labels: `コツ`, `秘訣`, `助言`, `アドバイス`, `豆知識`
- `warning`: Use for warning notices that are stronger than caution but not necessarily the strongest danger level.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-warning" role="doc-notice">`
  - English labels: `warning`, `warnings`
  - Japanese labels: `警告`, `警告事項`
  - Notes: Use for stronger warnings. Use `danger` only for the strongest hazard wording.

### Learning, Q&A, assessment, and evaluation

- `answer`: Use for answer text, especially a direct response to a question.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-answer">`
  - English labels: `answer`, `answers`
  - Japanese labels: `回答([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `答え([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`
  - Notes: Keep separate from `solution`: answers are responses, while solutions can include worked methods.
- `assessment`: Use for assessments, quizzes, exams, or diagnostic checks, not broad value or performance evaluations.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-assessment">`
  - English labels: `assessment`, `assessments`, `quiz`, `quizzes`, `exam`, `exams`
  - Japanese labels: `アセスメント`, `試験`, `小テスト`, `確認テスト`
  - Notes: `test` is intentionally not recognized because it is too broad in software documentation. This semantic covers assessment, quiz, exam, and diagnostic/checking sections. Bare Japanese `採点` is intentionally left out because scoring or grading is an action rather than an assessment itself. The singular canonical follows the current EPUB `assessment` term; plural `assessments` remains an input label.
- `evaluation`: Use for judgments of quality, value, effectiveness, results, or performance against criteria.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-evaluation">`
  - English labels: `evaluation`, `evaluations`, `product evaluation`, `product evaluations`, `quality evaluation`, `quality evaluations`, `performance evaluation`, `performance evaluations`
  - Japanese labels: `評価`, `評価結果`, `総合評価`, `製品評価`, `品質評価`, `性能評価`, `パフォーマンス評価`
  - Notes: Keep separate from `assessment`, `rubric`, and `feedback`: evaluation judges quality, value, effectiveness, results, or performance; assessment measures or tests; rubrics define criteria; feedback comments on results. Natural English product, quality, and performance evaluation headings are recognized in singular and plural forms. Japanese `評価`, `評価結果`, and `総合評価` are recognized as practical standalone headings because the roleless output does not assert an assessment or grading role. Assessment-like `リスク評価` and grading-like `成績評価` remain unregistered, while `評価基準` maps to `rubric`.
- `explanation`: Use for explanations, especially after questions, examples, procedures, or solutions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-explanation">`
  - English labels: `explanation`, `explanations`
  - Japanese labels: `解説`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted because there is no close DPUB-ARIA role.
- `faq`: Use for FAQ sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-faq" role="doc-qna">`
  - English labels: `faq`
  - Japanese labels: `ＦＡＱ`, `よくある(質問|問い合わせ)`
- `feedback`: Use for feedback sections or response/comments areas.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-feedback">`
  - English labels: `feedback`
  - Japanese labels: `フィードバック`
- `interview`: Use for interviews as an editorial content type, whether or not every passage follows a strict Q&A sequence.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-interview">`
  - English labels: `interview`
  - Japanese labels: `インタビュー`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted because an interview may include narrative, profile, or conversational material instead of a strict question-and-answer series. Use `qna` when the content is explicitly structured as questions and answers.
- `learning-objective`: Use for learning objectives or expected outcomes.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-learning-objective">`
  - English labels: `learning-objective`, `learning-objectives`, `learning objective`, `learning objectives`
  - Japanese labels: `学習目標`, `到達目標`
  - Notes: The singular canonical follows the current EPUB `learning-objective` term. Input aliases include `learning-objectives` and the natural spaced singular/plural headings. Bare `objectives` is intentionally not recognized because project and business objectives are not necessarily learning objectives. No default role is emitted because the EPUB term has no DPUB-ARIA role mapping.
- `lesson`: Use for lessons or learning units.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-lesson">`
  - English labels: `lesson`, `lessons`, `learning unit`, `learning units`
  - Japanese labels: `レッスン`, `単元`
- `problem`: Use for problems or exercises to be solved.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-problem">`
  - English labels: `problem`, `exercise`, `exercises`, `practice problem`, `practice problems`
  - Japanese labels: `問題([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `演習問題([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `練習問題([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`
  - Notes: A problem is a unit to be solved and may contain its prompt or instructions directly; it does not require a nested `question`. Questions, hints, answers, and explanations are independently usable blocks and are not linked automatically by matching numbers. `practice problem(s)` is recognized, but bare `practice` is intentionally not recognized. Japanese bare `問題` is retained for common exercise/problem headings; use `問題点` or `既知の問題` for an issue-reporting section.
- `qna`: Use for question-and-answer collections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-qna" role="doc-qna">`
  - English labels: `qna`, `Q&A`, `Q and A`, `Questions and answers`
  - Japanese labels: `Ｑ＆Ａ`, `質疑応答`, `一問一答`, `(問(題)?|質問)と(回答|答え)`
  - Notes: Bare `QA` is intentionally not recognized because technical documents commonly use it for quality assurance. Use `Q&A`, `Q and A`, or `Questions and answers` for a series of questions and answers; use `question` and `answer` for independent units.
- `question`: Use for individual questions or prompts.
  - Output: `<div class="sc-question">`
  - English labels: `question`
  - Japanese labels: `質問`, `問い([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `設問([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `小問([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `問[0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3}`, `発問([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `主発問`, `中心発問`
  - Notes: Keep separate from `problem`: a question is an independently labelled question, subquestion, or teaching prompt and may appear inside or outside a problem. Bare Japanese `問` stays unregistered, and specialist forms beyond the established `発問`, `主発問`, and `中心発問` labels remain conservative omissions.
- `rubric`: Use for grading rubrics or evaluation criteria.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-rubric">`
  - English labels: `rubric`, `grading rubric`
  - Japanese labels: `評価基準`, `採点基準`, `ルーブリック`
- `solution`: Use for solutions, worked answers, or methods of solving.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-solution">`
  - English labels: `solution`, `solutions`
  - Japanese labels: `解答([0-9０-９A-ZＡ-Ｚ一二三四五六七八九十]{1,3})?`, `解答例`, `解決(方法)?`, `解法`
  - Notes: Keep separate from `answer`: solutions may include reasoning or method.

### Technical and workflow documentation

- `check`: Use for checks, checklists, or confirmation items.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-check">`
  - English labels: `check`, `checklist`
  - Japanese labels: `チェック`, `確認事項`, `チェックリスト`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted because a checklist is a workflow section, not a DPUB notice by default.
- `decision`: Use for decisions or decided items in office, planning, or meeting documents.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-decision">`
  - English labels: `decision`, `decisions`
  - Japanese labels: `決定事項`, `決定内容`
- `issue`: Use for issues, points of dispute, or problems to discuss.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-issue">`
  - English labels: `issue`, `issues`, `known issue`, `known issues`
  - Japanese labels: `問題点`, `争点`, `論点`, `イシュー`, `検討課題`, `懸案事項`, `既知の問題`, `既知の問題点`
- `limitations`: Use for limitations, constraints, restrictions, or caveats.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-limitations">`
  - English labels: `limitations`, `limitation`, `constraints`, `constraint`, `restrictions`, `restriction`
  - Japanese labels: `制限事項`, `制約`, `制約事項`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted; this is a practical technical-document section rather than a DPUB structural role.
- `next-steps`: Use for follow-up actions or forward-looking next-step sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-next-steps">`
  - English labels: `next-steps`, `next steps`, `next step`
  - Japanese labels: `次のステップ`, `今後の対応`, `今後の予定`
  - Notes: Use `task` for specific assignments or action items, and `planning` for plans.
- `prerequisites`: Use for prerequisite knowledge, setup, or preconditions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-prerequisites">`
  - English labels: `prerequisites`, `prerequisite`
  - Japanese labels: `前提条件`, `事前準備`
- `procedure`: Use for procedures, steps, or instructions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-procedure">`
  - English labels: `procedure`, `procedures`, `steps`, `instructions`
  - Japanese labels: `手順`, `操作手順`, `作業手順`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted; use for how-to steps rather than requirements or resources.
- `requirements`: Use for requirements or conditions that must be satisfied.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-requirements">`
  - English labels: `requirements`, `requirement`, `system requirements`, `hardware requirements`, `software requirements`
  - Japanese labels: `要件`, `必要条件`, `必須要件`, `要求事項`, `必要事項`, `必須項目`, `動作要件`, `システム要件`, `動作環境`, `推奨環境`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted; use `procedure` for steps and `limitations` for restrictions. System, hardware, and software requirements map here. Japanese `推奨環境` also stays here because it describes an operating environment, not a general recommendation.
- `resources`: Use for resources, materials, handouts, or supporting assets.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-resources">`
  - English labels: `resources`, `resource`, `materials`
  - Japanese labels: `資料`, `教材`, `参考資料`
  - Notes: Use `bibliography` for formal citations and `related-link` for link-only see-also lists. Japanese `参考資料` maps here as supporting material rather than to singular `reference`.
- `task`: Use for tasks, assignments, homework, or action items.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-task">`
  - English labels: `task`, `tasks`, `assignment`, `assignments`, `homework`, `action item`, `action items`
  - Japanese labels: `タスク`, `宿題`, `アクションアイテム`
  - Notes: Includes school assignments and office action items.
- `troubleshooting`: Use for troubleshooting and diagnostic help sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-troubleshooting">`
  - English labels: `troubleshooting`
  - Japanese labels: `トラブルシューティング`, `困ったときは`
- `updates`: Use for update notes, revision history, or change-history sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-updates">`
  - English labels: `updates`, `revision history`, `change history`
  - Japanese labels: `(([0-9]+年)?[0-9]+月[0-9]+日)?更新`, `更新履歴`, `改訂履歴`
  - Notes: No default `role` is emitted. Use for general update notes or revision history; use `postscript` for added after-notes and `errata` for corrections.

### Editorial, planning, and related material

- `agenda`: Use for agenda sections, especially meeting agendas or lists of topics to discuss.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-agenda">`
  - English labels: `agenda`, `meeting agenda`
  - Japanese labels: `議題`, `アジェンダ`, `議事次第`
- `event`: Use for event information.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-event">`
  - English labels: `event`
  - Japanese labels: `イベント(情報|案内)?`, `行事(情報|案内)?`, `催し物(情報|案内)?`
- `minutes`: Use for meeting minutes or records of discussions and decisions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-minutes">`
  - English labels: `minutes`, `meeting minutes`
  - Japanese labels: `議事録`
- `opinion`: Use for opinion or viewpoint sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-opinion">`
  - English labels: `opinion`
  - Japanese labels: `意見`, `見解`, `オピニオン`
- `outline`: Use for outlines.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-outline">`
  - English labels: `outline`
  - Japanese labels: `概略`, `アウトライン`
- `overview`: Use for overviews.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-overview">`
  - English labels: `overview`
  - Japanese labels: `概要`, `概観`, `大要`, `あらまし`
  - Notes: Japanese `概要` can sometimes function as a formal abstract. It remains under the roleless `overview`; use `要旨` or `抄録` when the publication-specific `doc-abstract` meaning is intended.
- `planning`: Use for plans, planning sections, schedules, or intended courses of action.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-planning">`
  - English labels: `planning`, `plan`
  - Japanese labels: `計画`, `計画案`, `プラン`
  - Notes: Use `proposal` when a formal proposal or planning document is submitted for approval, and `suggestion` for a lighter idea.
- `profile`: Use for profile/person-introduction sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-profile">`
  - English labels: `profile`
  - Japanese labels: `プロフィール`, `人物紹介`
- `proposal`: Use for formal proposals or planning documents submitted for consideration, approval, or adoption.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-proposal">`
  - English labels: `proposal`, `project proposal`
  - Japanese labels: `プロポーザル`, `提案書`, `企画案`, `企画書`
  - Notes: Use for formal approval or adoption decisions. Japanese `提案書`, `企画案`, and `企画書` map here; bare `提案` maps to `suggestion`, and bare `企画` remains unregistered because it can mean planning more broadly.
- `recommendation`: Use for recommendations, advised choices, or advised actions.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-recommendation">`
  - English labels: `recommendation`, `recommendations`
  - Japanese labels: `勧告`, `提言`, `おすすめ`, `お勧め`, `推薦`, `推奨`, `推奨事項`, `推奨項目`, `リコメンド`
  - Notes: Use for advised choices or actions. Japanese `提言` maps here rather than to `proposal`; imperative or adjectival English forms such as `recommend` and `recommended` are intentionally not recognized as standalone labels.
- `reference`: Use for reference information or a single reference block.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-reference">`
  - English labels: `reference`
  - Japanese labels: `[レリ]ファレンス`, `参照`, `参照先`, `参照情報`
  - Notes: Use for a reference block, reference destination, or lookup information: content that identifies what or where to consult rather than merely presenting useful background information. Japanese `参照`, `参照先`, and `参照情報` map here; bare `参考` is intentionally not recognized because it is broad, `参考情報` maps to `information`, and supporting `参考資料` maps to `resources`.
- `related`: Use for broad related information.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-related">`
  - English labels: `related`, `related information`, `related resources`
  - Japanese labels: `関連情報`, `関連資料`
  - Notes: Use noun phrases such as `related information` and `related resources`; abstract `relation` is intentionally not recognized.
- `related-book`: Use for related book, magazine, or publication suggestions.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-related-book">`
  - English labels: `related-book`, `related book`, `related books`, `related magazine`, `related magazines`, `related-publication`, `related publication`, `related publications`
  - Japanese labels: `関連(した)?(本|書籍|雑誌|出版物|刊行物)`
  - Notes: This is the canonical semantic for related books, magazines, and other publications. `related-publication` and natural publication forms are input aliases; output and canonical-name configuration use `sc-related-book` / `related-book`.
- `related-article`: Use for related article suggestions.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-related-article">`
  - English labels: `related-article`, `related article`, `related articles`
  - Japanese labels: `関連(した)?記事`
- `related-link`: Use for related links, see-also links, or further-reading links.
  - Output: `<aside class="sc-related-link">`
  - English labels: `related-link`, `related link`, `related links`, `see also`, `further reading`
  - Japanese labels: `関連(した)?リンク`, `参考リンク`
  - Notes: Use `bibliography` for formal references lists; use this for navigational links.
- `supplement`: Use for supplements or supplemental information.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-supplement">`
  - English labels: `supplement`, `supplements`
  - Japanese labels: `補足(情報)?`, `補遺`
  - Notes: Use for material that clarifies or supplements the main work. Keep separate from `appendix`, which is a formal appended document structure and has the close `doc-appendix` role, and from a later addendum or postscript. Japanese `補遺` is retained as an established documentation term for a supplement. `追補` is closer to addendum and remains unregistered; `追記` stays under `postscript`.
- `suggestion`: Use for lighter suggestions that do not necessarily require formal approval.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-suggestion">`
  - English labels: `suggestion`, `suggestions`
  - Japanese labels: `提案`, `サジェスト`
  - Notes: Use for lighter ideas that do not necessarily require approval. Japanese bare `提案` maps here; formal `提案書` maps to `proposal`. English uses the noun labels `suggestion` and `suggestions`, not the verb `suggest`.
- `summary`: Use for summaries or recaps.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-summary">`
  - English labels: `summary`
  - Japanese labels: `要約`, `まとめ`, `あらすじ`
  - Notes: Japanese `まとめ` can also serve as a closing section. It remains under the roleless `summary`; use `結論` or `おわりに` when the stronger `doc-conclusion` meaning is intended.
- `topic`: Use for topics or subject sections.
  - Output: `<section class="sc-topic">`
  - English labels: `topic`
  - Japanese labels: `トピック`, `話題`
